title: Apostolic Authority and the One True Gospel

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — "Apostle—Not from Men nor by a Man"

heb.paragraph: The noun apostolos denotes one commissioned and sent with full authority to represent the sender. Paul’s emphatic denial that his apostleship derives ‘from men’ (ap’ anthrōpōn) or ‘by a man’ (di’ anthrōpou) establishes a double negation: neither the originating source nor the mediating channel of his commission is human. The prepositions apo (ultimate source) and dia (intermediate agency) are carefully distinguished, underscoring that Paul’s authority traces directly to the risen Christ and God the Father.

The term euangelion occurs seven times in Galatians (1:6, 7, 7, 11; 2:2, 5, 7, 14), more concentrated per line than in any other Pauline letter. In the Greco-Roman world the word denoted a public announcement of victory or an imperial proclamation. Paul appropriates it for the message of Christ’s death and resurrection, insisting there is only one authentic euangelion—any alternative is no gospel at all (1:6–7).

panels.ctx: Galatians opens with the most abrupt salutation in the Pauline corpus. Paul omits his customary thanksgiving and moves immediately to the crisis. The greeting contains a compressed creedal formula: Christ ‘gave himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age’ (1:4), framing the entire letter’s argument. The phrase ‘present evil age’ (tou aiōnos tou enestōtos ponērou) reflects Jewish apocalyptic categories: the current age is under the dominion of sin and death, and Christ’s self-giving inaugurates the age to come.

cross.note: Paul’s foundational creedal summary—‘Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures’—parallels the compressed christological formula in Gal 1:4, confirming that Paul’s gospel in Galatians is identical to the apostolic kerygma.
The language of Christ giving himself ‘to redeem us from all wickedness’ echoes Gal 1:4, using similar sacrificial and redemptive vocabulary rooted in the Exodus tradition.

notes.note: Paul’s opening salvo establishes the divine origin of his apostleship against the Judaizers’ claim that he was merely a secondary delegate. The phrase ‘not from men nor by a man’ repudiates both the ultimate source and the instrumental agency of any human commissioning. Paul was called directly by the risen Christ on the Damascus road, making his authority equal to the Twelve.

The greeting’s creedal core—Christ ‘gave himself for our sins’—is substitutionary atonement in condensed form. The voluntary nature of Christ’s sacrifice and its purpose (‘to rescue us from the present evil age’) establish that salvation is accomplished entirely by divine initiative, not by human works or law-keeping.

The doxology ‘to whom be glory for ever and ever’ anchors the letter’s argument in the glory of God. If justification came through the law, the glory would belong partly to human effort. Because salvation is entirely God’s work through Christ, all glory belongs to God alone.

Paul claims to be an apostle ‘not from men nor by a man, but by Jesus Christ and God the Father.’ This is not mere formality; it is the hinge on which the entire epistle turns. The false teachers undermined Paul’s gospel by undermining his credentials. Calvin observes that the order matters: Paul names Christ before the Father not to subordinate the Father, but because Christ is the immediate agent of his calling.

The phrase ‘gave himself for our sins’ contains the whole doctrine of redemption in miniature. Calvin notes that this voluntary self-giving excludes all human merit: if Christ’s sacrifice is sufficient to rescue from the present evil age, then the Judaizers’ insistence on circumcision is not merely unnecessary but insulting to the cross.

The prepositional contrast between apo (source) and dia (agency) is theologically precise. Paul denies human origin at every level. The NET translators note that ‘through Jesus Christ and God the Father’ coordinates Christ and the Father as joint agents, implying functional equality.

The aorist participle ‘who gave himself’ (tou dontos heauton) points to the definitive, once-for-all character of Christ’s sacrifice. The purpose clause uses exelētai (aorist subjunctive of exaireō), a term with deliverance connotations drawn from the LXX Exodus tradition.

Paul’s salutation is polemical from the first word. The emphatic denial of human origin for his apostleship responds directly to the agitators’ charge that Paul is a derivative apostle. Moo notes that the christological formula in v. 4 is pre-Pauline traditional material that Paul cites because it is common ground. The phrase ‘present evil age’ locates the argument within apocalyptic eschatology—Christ’s death has inaugurated a new epoch, and to return to Torah observance as the basis of right standing is to deny the eschatological newness of the Christ-event.

Bruce emphasises that Paul’s double negation (ouk ... oude) followed by the strong adversative alla creates a tripartite structure: not from men, not through a man, but through Jesus Christ and God the Father. The coordination of Christ and the Father under a single preposition (dia) without the repetition of the article is grammatically significant, pointing to their unity of action. The reference to the resurrection is not incidental: it was the risen Christ who appeared to Paul on the Damascus road, and it is this resurrection appearance that constitutes his apostolic credential (cf. 1 Cor 9:1; 15:8).

heb.paragraph: The verb metatithēmi means to transfer allegiance or change sides. In political discourse it described defection to an enemy. Paul uses the present tense, indicating the desertion is in progress but not yet complete—there is still time to return.

The term anathema derives from the LXX translation of Hebrew ḥērem (the ban of total destruction). Paul invokes the curse twice (vv. 8–9), intensifying the severity: even an apostle or an angel from heaven who preaches a different gospel falls under divine judgment.

panels.ctx: Paul’s astonishment (thaumazō) that the Galatians are ‘so quickly’ (houtōs tacheōs) deserting is without parallel in his letters. The typical Pauline thanksgiving section is replaced by rebuke. Paul insists that the rival teaching is not merely a variant interpretation but a heteron euangelion—a ‘different gospel’ that is in fact no gospel at all (ho ouk estin allo). The distinction between heteros (different in kind) and allos (another of the same kind) underscores Paul’s point: there is one authentic gospel, and any deviation from it is a perversion (metastrepsai).

cross.note: Paul warns the Corinthians against accepting ‘a different gospel,’ using nearly identical language to Gal 1:6–7. Both passages address the same fundamental danger: rival missionaries distorting the apostolic message.
The prohibition against adding to or subtracting from God’s commands provides the Old Testament background for Paul’s insistence that the gospel admits no supplementation.

notes.note: The absence of a thanksgiving section is itself a rebuke. In every other letter, Paul finds something to commend before correcting. Here the situation is too urgent. The Galatians are not drifting into minor error but abandoning the gospel itself.

The double anathema is the strongest possible language. Paul places himself and even angels under the same standard: the message is more authoritative than the messenger. This principle demolishes any appeal to ecclesiastical authority or church tradition that contradicts the apostolic gospel.

Paul’s rhetorical question—‘Am I now trying to win the approval of human beings, or of God?’—answers the charge that he trims his message to please his audience. People-pleasing and Christ-serving are mutually exclusive vocations.

Calvin observes that Paul does not say ‘you are turning away from the gospel’ but ‘from him who called you by the grace of Christ.’ Departure from the gospel is departure from God himself. Calvin insists that grace mixed with works is no longer grace. The anathema applies to those who corrupt the article of justification on which the church stands or falls.

The accusation that Paul was a people-pleaser reveals the Judaizers’ strategy. Calvin notes the irony: it is the Judaizers who are people-pleasers, preaching circumcision to avoid persecution (6:12). Paul’s willingness to curse even an angel demonstrates that his concern is truth, not popularity.
The verb metatithesthe (present middle/passive) indicates a process in progress, not a completed act. The NET translators render it ‘deserting’ to capture the military connotation of defection.

The shift from the hypothetical ean (v. 8, with subjunctive) to the factual ei (v. 9, with indicative) sharpens the application from the theoretical to the actual: someone is in fact preaching a contrary gospel.

Moo argues that the absence of the thanksgiving is theological: Paul cannot give thanks for a community in the process of apostasy. The double anathema (vv. 8–9) establishes the objective standard of the gospel against which all teaching must be measured. Moo notes the eschatological overtone: anathema places the offender under the final curse of God.

Bruce notes that houtōs tacheōs is ambiguous: it could mean quickly after Paul’s departure, quickly after the agitators’ arrival, or too readily. The double curse formula has parallels in Deuteronomic covenant curses and underscores the covenantal seriousness of the gospel.

sections.header: Verses 11–24 — "Received by Revelation, Not Human Instruction"

heb.paragraph: The noun apokalypsis denotes the uncovering of what was previously hidden. Paul insists his gospel came through an ‘apocalypse of Jesus Christ’ (apokalypseōs Iēsou Christou, v. 12)—a divine disclosure, not human tradition. The genitive is likely both subjective (Christ reveals) and objective (Christ is revealed), as confirmed by v. 16: God revealed his Son ‘in me’ (en emoi).

Ioudaismos appears only here and in 2 Maccabees in biblical literature. It denotes the entire cultural-religious complex of Second Temple Jewish identity. Paul’s statement that he ‘was advancing in Judaism beyond many of my own age’ (v. 14) presents his pre-conversion life as the apex of covenantal zeal.

panels.ctx: Paul’s autobiographical narrative in vv. 11–24 serves a polemical function: by demonstrating that he received the gospel by direct revelation rather than human mediation, he refutes the charge that he is a second-hand apostle. The narrative moves through three stages: (1) his pre-conversion life as a zealous persecutor (vv. 13–14); (2) his call and commission, described in language echoing Jeremiah 1:5 and Isaiah 49:1; (3) his deliberate avoidance of Jerusalem for three years, going instead to Arabia and Damascus (vv. 15–17). His brief, fifteen-day visit to Peter (v. 18) and his subsequent invisibility to the Judean churches (vv. 22–24) underscore his independence from human authority.

cross.note: God’s declaration to Jeremiah provides the prophetic template for Paul’s self-understanding. The parallels in Gal 1:15 are deliberate, presenting Paul’s apostolic call in continuity with Israel’s prophetic tradition.
Luke’s account of the Damascus road experience provides the narrative framework for the revelation Paul describes in Gal 1:12, 15–16.
Paul’s catalogue of Jewish credentials parallels and expands the autobiographical data in Gal 1:13–14, and in both contexts Paul counts these credentials as loss.

notes.note: Paul’s assertion that his gospel is ‘not of human origin’ is the epistemological foundation of apostolic authority. This claim is either true or Paul is a liar; there is no middle ground.
Paul’s pre-conversion credentials were impeccable. The point is devastating to the Judaizers: Paul knows the law better than they do, and he has found it to be a dead end for justification.
The echo of Jeremiah 1:5 and Isaiah 49:1 is deliberate. God did not consult human beings before calling the prophets, and he did not consult the Jerusalem apostles before calling Paul.

Paul’s visit to Peter lasted only fifteen days. The Judean churches knew Paul only by reputation: ‘The man who formerly persecuted us is now preaching the faith he once tried to destroy.’ Their response was to praise God, not to claim credit.
Calvin underscores the significance of Paul’s solemn assertion: the gospel he preaches was not received by human tradition nor taught by human instruction but came through divine revelation.

The language of being ‘set apart from birth’ locates Paul’s vocation in God’s eternal decree. Calvin sees here the doctrine of effectual calling: God’s purpose preceded Paul’s existence, and his calling was an act of sovereign grace.
The phrase di’ apokalypseōs Iēsou Christou is grammatically ambiguous. The NET translators favour a combination of subjective and objective, noting that v. 16 clarifies: God revealed ‘his Son in me.’
The verb historēsai is a hapax legomenon in the NT. It connotes inquiry or investigation, suggesting Paul visited Peter to get acquainted rather than to receive instruction.

Moo reads the section as a carefully structured argument with three phases: (1) pre-call zeal as a persecutor; (2) unmediated divine revelation; (3) deliberate avoidance of Jerusalem. The echoes of prophetic call narratives are significant, placing Paul’s commission in the same salvation-historical category as the prophets.

Bruce observes that Paul’s narrative is a historical argument designed to establish one point: his gospel came from God, not from Jerusalem. The three stages form a chiastic pattern with the revelation as the centre and turning-point. Bruce notes the technical use of historēsai: in classical Greek it means to inquire or investigate.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as Paul grounds his apostleship in God’s unilateral call and eternal purpose. Judgment is prominent through the double anathema. Mission is central as Paul recounts his commission to preach Christ among the Gentiles.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Salutation: apostle by divine commission, not human appointment

rows.label: vv.3–5
rows.text: Grace and peace: Christ gave himself to rescue from this evil age

rows.label: vv.6–9
rows.text: Rebuke: deserting the gospel; double anathema on any rival message

rows.label: v.10
rows.text: Transition: Paul serves Christ, not human opinion

rows.label: vv.11–12
rows.text: Thesis: the gospel came by revelation, not human transmission

rows.label: vv.13–14
rows.text: Pre-call: zealous persecutor, advancing in Judaism

rows.label: vv.15–17
rows.text: The call: set apart from birth, revelation of the Son, Arabia

rows.label: vv.18–20
rows.text: First Jerusalem visit: fifteen days with Peter, oath of truthfulness

rows.label: vv.21–24
rows.text: Unknown in Judea; the churches glorify God

lit.note: Galatians 1 establishes Paul’s apostolic credentials through a three-stage autobiographical argument: his pre-conversion zeal, his unmediated divine call, and his post-call independence from Jerusalem. The chapter’s literary centre is the double anathema (vv. 8–9), which functions as the thesis of the entire letter.

hebtext.note: The noun apostolos denotes one commissioned and sent with full authority to represent the sender. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: The greeting contains a compressed creedal formula: Christ ‘gave himself for our sins to rescue us from the present evil age’ (1:4), framing the entire letter’s argument…

ppl.name: Peter
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon bar Jonah) dominates Acts 1–12 as the church’s primary spokesman and miracle-worker. He preaches at Pentecost (Acts 2), heals at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3), defends before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4–5), raises Tabitha (Acts 9:36–42), and receives the vision that opens the gospel to Gentiles (Acts 10). His release from Herod’s prison by an angel (Acts 12) closes his narrative in Acts; Paul’s story takes over from ch.13.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Apostolic Authority and the One True Gospel

translations.text: which is really no gospel at all. Evidently some people are throwing you into confusion and are trying to pervert the gospel of Christ.
not that there is another one, but there are some who trouble you and want to distort the gospel of Christ.
I am astonished that you are so quickly deserting the one who called you to live in the grace of Christ and are turning to a different gospel—
I am astonished that you are so quickly deserting him who called you in the grace of Christ and are turning to a different gospel—

debate: The prepositional contrast between apo (source) and dia (agency) is theologically precise. Paul denies human origin at every level. The NET translator…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Apostolic Authority and the One True Gospel within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
